summary:
St. Peter's Basilica (construction 1506–1626 AD) is the largest Christian church in the world and the most important building in Catholicism — a 120-year construction project in the heart of the [[event:101|Vatican]] that consumed the work of the greatest architects of the Renaissance and Baroque periods (Bramante, Raphael, Michelangelo, Maderno, Bernini), and whose fund-raising indirectly triggered the [[event:102|Protestant Reformation]]. Pope Julius II, who laid the foundation stone in 1506, decided to rebuild the ancient basilica of Constantine (which stood over what tradition identified as the tomb of St. Peter) into the grandest church the world had ever seen — a project of breathtaking ambition that would require the entire 16th and early 17th centuries to complete. The sale of indulgences to fund the basilica's construction was among the specific grievances that prompted Martin Luther's [[event:2430|95 Theses]] in 1517.

Michelangelo, appointed chief architect in 1546 at age 72, designed the magnificent dome (143 meters high at the cross) that dominates the Roman skyline and became the architectural model for domes worldwide — from St. Paul's in London to the US Capitol in Washington. Gian Lorenzo Bernini designed the sweeping elliptical colonnade of St. Peter's Square (1656–1667), creating one of the world's great urban spaces and the symbolic embrace of the Catholic Church. The basilica's interior, with its overwhelming scale (capable of holding 60,000 people), Bernini's towering bronze baldachin over the papal altar, and Michelangelo's Pietà, remains the physical center of world Catholicism. The construction of St. Peter's thus represents both the height of Renaissance-Baroque artistic achievement and one of history's most consequential examples of unintended historical consequences.

Indulgences sale St Peters funding Protestant Reformation trigger
